kids.text: This is just lcamtuf's font from 2009. Nothing new here, just repackaged for the microcontroller nostalgia crowd. We were doing this on the Vic-20.

comments.text:
I appreciate the effort here, but this feels like the kind of post that gets submitted to Show HN because the author is excited about shipping something, not because anyone actually needs a 5x5 font. The real question nobody's asking is: who is building things on AVR128DA28 with a 160x128 OLED and hitting memory constraints so tight that 350 bytes of font data matters, but loose enough that they're rendering arbitrary text?

Most embedded projects either (a) use fixed strings baked into flash, in which case a bitmap of exactly what you need is smaller, or (b) have enough resources to use a proper font renderer like FreeType, which solves the actual problem (kerning, scaling, ligatures, unicode).

lcamtuf's work was genuinely useful because he was solving a specific constraint at AFL Fuzzer's scale. This feels more like a leetcode problem dressed up as a practical tool. The ZX Spectrum reference is charming nostalgia, but nostalgia isn't a use case.

If you've actually shipped this in something real, I'd genuinely love

kids.text: AVR128DA28 has 16 kB of RAM, not flash. The font data lives in flash (program memory), which is 128 kB on that chip. So the 350 bytes doesn't actually compete with display framebuffer space at all. The post conflates the two

I actually think this solves a real constraint, just not the one you're framing. The tight memory budget matters less than the predictable rendering model. If you're driving a small display from an AVR or STM32, you care about:

1. Constant width means you can compute string bounds in one multiplication instead of iterating through a lookup table.
2. At 5x5, you can fit 32x25 characters on a 160x128 display, which is enough for a small menu or status readout.
3. 350 bytes is nothing, but more importantly, the entire font fits in L1 cache on devices with caches, and the algorithm to rasterize it is branchless.

The actual use case is probably "I'm building a smart thermostat / industrial controller
lcamtuf's font-inline.h is excellent but this 5x5 variant is cleaner for OLEDs. If you're in this space, also check out Adafruit's GFX library fonts (especially the 5x7 bitmap set) and TinyFont

comments.text:
I think the framing here misses the real win. This isn't about "how small can a font get", it's about accepting that most embedded display work doesn't need full Unicode or variable width at all.

Once you stop treating it as a font design problem and start treating it as a bandwidth/storage problem, suddenly a 350-byte fixed-width bitmap makes perfect sense. You're not optimizing for aesthetics, you're optimizing for the constraint that your AVR128DA28 or STM32L has maybe 16KB of addressable memory and a 128x64 OLED.

The policy angle here is interesting too: there's a regulatory push in IoT to ship devices with lower power budgets and minimal flash footpr

kids.text: I built a scrolling status display on an STM32L last year with a 128x64 OLED and honestly this 5x5 approach would have saved me weeks. I was trying to cram a proportional font and kept running out of RAM for the actual application logic. Switched
Weeks? You were doing it wrong then. Fixed width bitmaps have been the obvious choice for embedded displays since, I dunno, forever. This isn't some revelation.

comments.text: Just tested this on an Arduino with a 128x64 OLED. Compiled to 412 bytes with the full ASCII set, which is honestly smaller than I expected. Rendering speed is instant even at 60 FPS. The 5x5 constraint actually makes the glyphs

kids.text: This reminds me of the tiny bitmap fonts on old graphing calculators in the 90s. I had a TI-85 and spent way too long staring at those 5x7 glyphs. The tradeoff between readability and memory footprint was always brutal on those devices
If you're into this space, check out Tom7's work on embedded fonts - his "Font of Interesting Sizes" has some clever 4x6 and 5x7 variants that trade off differently. https://tom7.org/grad/fonts/

comments.text: The 350-byte footprint is the real win here. Most embedded projects I've worked on (weather stations, sensor arrays, old Kindle hacks) hit a hard RAM wall around 16-32KB. A monospace font that trades zero design fluff for pure utility tends to outperform the prettier alternatives once you factor in the bitmap decompression overhead.

kids.text:
This reminds me of the constraint I hit working on firmware for some ancient Casio calculators with 2KB of RAM. We ended up embedding a 3x5 bitmap font in about 80 bytes, but the trade-off was brutal-lowercase letters were basically unreadable, and kerning was completely impossible.

The jump to 5x5 actually solves real problems. The 350 bytes is almost negligible on modern microcontrollers, but the consistency of fixed-width makes display code so much simpler. I've seen projects spend weeks

I hit that same wall on a Mars Rover emulator project at uni (borrowed code from Russ Cox's Plan 9 font work). We needed to display telemetry on a 128x64 LCD and the fixed-width constraint was a lifesaver for the buffer calculations.

The irony is that we've spent 40 years optimizing fonts for screens with megabytes of RAM, then act surprised when embedded systems need to reinvent 1980s solutions. This is solid work, but it's almost a referendum on how bloated our tooling has become.

comments.text:
This is just lcamtuf's 5x6 font with one pixel shaved off and presented as some grand optimization. The whole premise of "5x5 is the minimum" falls apart the second you try to render anything below 20pt on an actual screen. Most people can't even read 4x4 clearly, let alone rely on it for anything mission-critical.

And the claim about memory efficiency is misleading. Yes, 350 bytes sounds tiny, but you're already burning 110KB+ on the framebuffer for any reasonable display. Shaving 350 bytes doesn't meaningfully change your project constraints. If you're actually RAM-constrained to that degree, you're not rendering fonts at all, you're bit-shifting pre-rendered glyphs.

The monospace justification ("length of a string is always 6 times the number of characters") is fine but that's not new. Every bitmap font does this. The constant-width thing is a solved problem that doesn't need rehashing.

kids.text: The 5x5 constraint is actually more subtle than just shaving a pixel. The monospace fixed-width grid (6 chars per 6 pixels) matters a lot for embedded work where you're compositing text into pre-calculated layouts. On a 128x64 OLED,

If you're targeting microcontrollers with tight constraints, there's a reason bitmap fonts like this still dominate over vector approaches. That said, for anything consumer-facing on embedded displays, you'd want to look at what folks in the IoT space are already doing-projects like LVGL use adaptive scaling precisely because a fixed 5x5 assumes your use case is either hobby-level or pre-constrained to very specific hardware. The real win
